coredata.dc:title: A carbon dot-based time-dependent color-changing room temperature phosphorescent material
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Le
Wang, Yang
Bian, Zhentao
Komarneni, Sridhar
Hu, Guangzhou
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) based room-temperature phosphorescent materials have received extensive research attention due to their excellent optical properties, low biotoxicity and their great potential for applications in the field of anti-counterfeiting. However, only few CDs-based materials possessing time-dependent color-changing room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) properties have been reported. In the present work, RTP composite materials with unique time-dependent color-changing properties were synthesized by a one-pot method using ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), boric acid (BA) and phosphoric acid (PA) as raw materials. The phosphorus-doped composites of carbon dots and boric acid (P-CDs@BA) possess yellow and green RTP properties. The coexistence of multiple luminescent centers and the difference in the phosphor lifetime and luminescence intensity of the luminescent centers make the P-CDs@BA composites possess unique time-dependent color change properties. Finally, a specific example of the application of P-CDs@BA composites in the field of anti-counterfeiting is given based on their time-dependent color-changing RTP properties. The present work provides ideas for the preparation of time-dependent color-changing carbon dot-based room temperature phosphorescent materials and their applications in the field of anti-counterfeiting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Time-dependence
Color-change
Carbon dots
Room temperature phosphorescence
Anti-counterfeiting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of P-CDs@BA composite anti-counterfeiting ink and drawing of anti-counterfeiting patterns
Morphological and chemical structural characterization of P-CDs@BA composites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis scheme for P-CDs@BA composites and anti-counterfeit ink.
(a) TEM images, (b) particle size distribution and (c) lower magnification TEM images of P-CDs@BA and (d) TEM images, (e) particle size distribution and (f) lower magnification TEM images of CDs@BA.

(a) XPS spectra of P-CDs@BA, P-CDs and CDs@BA, (b) Raman spectrum of P-CDs@BA, (c) FT-IR spectra of BA, P-CDs@BA, P-CDs and CDs@BA, (d) full XPS spectra of P-CDs@BA (e) N1s, (f) B1s and (g) P 2p high-resolution XPS spectra.
Photographs of P-CDs@BA powder taken with UV lamp (365 nm) on and off for 0–4 s.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of P-CDs@BA and CDs@BA; (b), (c) their fluorescence spectra under different wavelengths of excitation; (d), (e) their phosphorescence spectra under different wavelength excitations; and (f) the color CIE 1931 coordinates for the variation of P-CDs@BA with time.
(a), (b) RTP lifetime decay curves of P-CDs@BA (λex = 335 nm，λem = 525 nm) and P-CDs@BA (λex = 365 nm，λem = 573 nm), and (c) time-resolved phosphorescence spectra of P-CDs@BA under 335 nm excitation.
Demonstration of P-CDs@BA multi-level anti-counterfeiting application.
Comparison table for P-CDs@BA compared with the previous report.
Fitting parameters of RTP decay curves of P-CDs@BA.

head.ce:title: A carbon dot-based time-dependent color-changing room temperature phosphorescent material

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemical Technology, Institute of Chemical Technology, China University of Mining &Technology, Xuzhou, Jiangsu, 221116, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemical Technology, Institute of Chemical Technology, China University of Mining &Technology, Xuzhou, Jiangsu 221116, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Materials Research Institute and Department of Ecosystem Science and Management, 204 Energy and the Environment Laboratory, The Pennsylvania State University, University Park, PA, 16802, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Materials Research Institute and Department of Ecosystem Science and Management

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Materials Research Institute and Department of Ecosystem Science and Management, 204 Energy and the Environment Laboratory, The Pennsylvania State University, University Park, PA 16802, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) based room-temperature phosphorescent materials have received extensive research attention due to their excellent optical properties, low biotoxicity and their great potential for applications in the field of anti-counterfeiting. However, only few CDs-based materials possessing time-dependent color-changing room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) properties have been reported. In the present work, RTP composite materials with unique time-dependent color-changing properties were synthesized by a one-pot method using ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), boric acid (BA) and phosphoric acid (PA) as raw materials. The phosphorus-doped composites of carbon dots and boric acid (P-CDs@BA) possess yellow and green RTP properties. The coexistence of multiple luminescent centers and the difference in the phosphor lifetime and luminescence intensity of the luminescent centers make the P-CDs@BA composites possess unique time-dependent color change properties. Finally, a specific example of the application of P-CDs@BA composites in the field of anti-counterfeiting is given based on their time-dependent color-changing RTP properties. The present work provides ideas for the preparation of time-dependent color-changing carbon dot-based room temperature phosphorescent materials and their applications in the field of anti-counterfeiting.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are considered a new type of zero-dimensional nanomaterial [1,2]. Since its discovery in 2004 [3], CDs have received a lot of attention due to their excellent optical properties, easy preparation process, excellent biocompatibility, low biotoxicity and environmental friendliness [4–9]. Because of these properties, CDs have great potential for applications in optoelectronic devices, photocatalysis, food packaging, anti-counterfeiting, information encryption and bioimaging [10–19]. Researchers have begun to investigate the mechanism and preparation of fluorescent properties of CDs extensively, but the short fluorescence lifetime compared to phosphorescence greatly limits the possibilities of CDs in some aspects.

The first carbon dot-based room temperature phosphorescent materials material was reported in 2013 when Zhao and his colleagues prepared CDs@PVA composites by embedding the prepared CDs in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to achieve RTP emission from carbon dot-based materials [20]. Since then, the properties and preparation of carbon dot-based room temperature phosphorescent materials have been studied, and many carbon dot-based room-temperature phosphorescent materials have been developed and explored. This has opened up more possibilities for the application of CDs. Due to the diverse synthesis methods of CDs, raw material diversity and more complex structure, there is no clear and unified theory on the mechanism of photoluminescence properties of carbon dots [21,22]. For carbon dot-based room temperature phosphorescent materials, both efficient intersystem crossing (ISC) process and low non-radiative dissipation of the triplet excited state are important. Currently, there are two main strategies for the preparation of carbon dot-based room-temperature phosphorescent materials: by introducing heteroatoms, such as sulfur, phosphorus, halogen ions and metal ions, etc., where inter-system scattering and lowering of energy gaps are promoted, resulting in carbon-dot-based RTP materials with long wavelength emission [23–26]; carbon dot-based room temperature phosphorescent materials are obtained by embedding carbon dots in a matrix, such as boric acid, urea, silica, dicyandiamide, and polymers, among others, which will stabilize the triple excited state by reducing nonradiative dissipation through spatial confinement and providing a rigidizing environment [27–32]. When the triple excited state energy levels of the donor and acceptor are well matched in the donor-acceptor system, energy transfer can occur, and thus red-shifting of the emission wavelength can be achieved [33]. In addition, due to the possible existence of multiple luminescent centers in CDs [34,35], time-dependent color-changing photoluminescence and excitation-dependent photoluminescence can be achieved, and more possibilities for applications such as ratiometric probes are also available [36]. As shown in Table 1 , there are many reports of multicolored room temperature phosphorescent carbon dots, and the reported dots emit light of many different colors ranging from blue to red, and some report a set of carbon dots with tunable colors. However, few carbon-dot-based time-dependent color-changing room-temperature phosphorescent materials have been reported so far. Therefore, the development of carbon dot-based room-temperature phosphorescent materials with time-dependent color change characteristics is still a great challenge.

In the present work, a carbon dot-based room-temperature phosphorescent material was successfully prepared by the one-pot method using EDTA as the carbon source and adding PA and BA to it as a matrix. It should be noted that this material's room temperature phosphorescence emission has the special property of time-dependent color-changing. The two strategies of doping the heteroatoms and embedding the matrix synergistically enable the prepared carbon dot-based room temperature phosphorescent materials to obtain time-dependent color-changing properties of phosphorescence from yellow to green. Aiming at the unique properties of the prepared materials, we try to apply them in the field of anti-counterfeiting, through its unique time-dependent color-changing room temperature phosphorescent properties, we can achieve the purpose of multi-level anti-counterfeiting, and its dynamic color-changing behaviors will become another important anti-counterfeiting means in addition to the means of patterns and phosphorescent colors. The present work provides ideas for the preparation of time-dependent color-changing carbon dot-based room temperature phosphorescent materials and their applications in the field of anti-counterfeiting.

The EDTA and BA used in the experiments were purchased from Shanghai Maclean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. and PA from Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co. All chemicals used in the experiments were purchased from commercial companies and used directly without any further purification process.

As shown in Fig. 1 , 0.4 g of EDTA (0.001 mol) and 1.5 g of BA (0.024 mol) were weighed and mixed by thorough grinding in an agate mortar and then transferred to a beaker. After adding 5 mL of ultrapure water to the beaker, the beaker was sonicated in an ultrasonic digestion bath and 0.4 mL of PA was added dropwise to the beaker, followed by placing the beaker in a blast drying oven and heating to 200 °C for 6 h. After the reaction was complete, the reactants were allowed to cool naturally to room temperature. The final yellow powder obtained is the P-CDs@BA composites.

Removal of BA or phosphoric acid PA from the reactants and prepared by the same procedure as above produced phosphorus-doped matrix-free CDs (P-CDs) and composites of phosphorus-free CDs and BA matrix (CDs@BA), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of P-CDs@BA composite anti-counterfeiting ink and drawing of anti-counterfeiting patterns
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Figs. 1 and 1.4 g of P-CDs@BA composites were added to 5 mL of anhydrous ethanol. Then, the mixed P-CDs@BA composites and anhydrous ethanol were sonicated at room temperature for 10 min (420 W, 40 KHz) to make the P-CDs@BA composites dispersed in the anhydrous ethanol as evenly as possible to prepare the anti-counterfeiting ink.

As shown in Fig. 8, using P-CDs@BA composite ink, we drew the butterfly and floral patterns by drawing them through missed cardboard prints. The anti-counterfeiting ink is evenly coated onto the paper surface, waiting for the solvent to evaporate to obtain the anti-counterfeiting patterns.

UV-V is spectrum was measured by UV-1800 UV–Vis spectrometer. Phosphorescence emission spectra and phosphorescence lifetime decay maps were obtained using an Edinburgh Instruments spectrometer (FLS1000). Time-resolved phosphorescence spectra were recorded at room temperature by an Edinburgh FLS5 spectrofluorometer equipped with a μs flash lamp (μF900). Phosphorescence-related data were measured at room temperature using Edinburgh FLS5 and FLS1000 spectrofluorometers. Data related to the fluorescence emission spectra of the materials were obtained by testing with a fluorescence spectrometer FS5. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained using a JEOL JEM-2100F field emission transmission electron microscope manufactured in Japan. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were acquired by NICOLET IS5 from Thermo Fisher, USA. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of polycrystalline materials were measured by using an X-ray diffractometer, model D8 ADVANCE. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out using a Thermo Fisher Nexsa from Thermo Fisher, USA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and chemical structural characterization of P-CDs@BA composites
ce:section.ce:para: The morphological characteristics of P-CDs@BA and CDs@BA composites were characterized by TEM. Sphere-like nanoparticles were prepared and they could be seen by high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) along with their distinct crystalline structure with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm (Fig. 2 a), which was attributed to the (100) face of graphite [37]. As shown by the particle size distribution of P-CDs@BA (Fig. 2b), their average particle size is 5.57 nm. Similarly, as shown in Fig. 2d and e, there are also sphere-like nanoparticles in CDs@BA with a lattice spacing of 0.23 nm and an average particle size of 4.25 nm. The morphological phase structure and particle size characteristics of the two are similar. By further reducing the magnification, a uniform distribution of nanoparticles in the matrix can be observed from the TEM images (Fig. 2c, f) of P-CDs@BA and CDs@BA. The presence of the matrix will cause a spatial confinement effect and will also act as a barrier to oxygen, thus being able to inhibit non-radiative decay due to the movement, collision, aggregation, and oxygen burst of CDs [38].

To further investigate the chemical structure of P-CDs@BA composites, XRD, Raman，FT-IR and XPS were determined to characterize P-CDs@BA, P-CDs and CDs@BA. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the XRD patterns of P-CDs@BA, P-CDs and CDs@BA showed similar peaks in all three. However, it is noteworthy that new peaks located at 14.7° and 40° appear in the XRD patterns of P-CDs@BA and CDs@BA composites compared to P-CDs, and these peaks correspond to B2O3 [27,39]. The above results show that we successfully prepared P-CDs@BA composites by embedding CDs in the borate matrix after adding BA.

We further performed Raman analysis to explore the structural properties of the synthesized P-CDs@BA. As shown in Fig. 3b, two peaks are observed at 1371.53 and 1601.88 cm− 1, which are ascribed to the disordered or defective carbon (D band) and the well-organized graphitic carbon (G band) of the P-CDs@BA, respectively [30,31]. The intensity ratio of D band and G band (ID/IG) was calculated to be 0.83. This indicates the presence of a large amount of graphitic carbon in P-CDs@BA. Meanwhile, P-CDs@BA also has defects.

As shown in Fig. 3c, the FT-IR spectra of P-CDs@BA and CDs@BA retained the characteristics of BA spectra. Further analysis showed that the FT-IR spectra of P-CDs@BA and CDs@BA have a broad absorption band between 2868 cm−1 and 3748 cm−1, which can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of O–H/N–H (3228 cm−1) and C–H (3008 cm−1) [40,41]. The absorption peak located at 1686 cm−1 is attributed to the CN stretching vibration [42]. In addition, the absorption peaks at 1438 cm−1, 1191 cm−1 and 950 cm−1 can be attributed to the B–O/C–N stretching vibration, the B–O–H bending vibration and the B–C stretching vibration, respectively [27,43,44]. The hydrogen bonding between the surface groups of the carbon dots, between the surface groups and the substrate, and within the substrate can provide a rigidized environment for the carbon dots to suppress the vibrational rotation of the groups, which will suppress the nonradiative decay of the triplet excited state and ultimately promote the emission of room temperature phosphorescence. It is noteworthy that absorption peaks located around 1089 cm−1 appear in the FT-IR spectra of P-CDs@BA and P-CDs compared to CDs@BA, which can be attributed to the P–O/PO stretching vibration [45]. Meanwhile, a smaller broad absorption band appears in the FT-IR spectra of P-CDs@BA between 882 cm−1 and 962 cm−1, which contains an absorption located at 917 cm−1, due to the stretching vibration of P–N [46,47]. It is speculated that the vibration of P–N may form a new luminescence center. The doping of phosphorus element will facilitate the electron spin-orbit coupling of carbon dots and close the energy level gap to enhance the chance of ISC making the emission wavelength red-shifted, and finally achieve the long wavelength room temperature phosphorescence emission [38]. The above results further indicate that we have successfully prepared composites with phosphorus-doped CDs embedded in the boric acid matrix, which are centered on sphere-like carbon cores and contain abundant groups on the surface.

From the (XPS) full spectra (Fig. 3d), it can be observed that the key elemental compositions of the P-CDs@BA composites are C, O, N, B and P (24.54 %, 39.23 %, 2.45 %, 30.73 % and 3.05 %, respectively), corresponding to the peaks located at 285.52 eV, 533.52 eV, 401.52 eV, 193.52 eV and 135.52 eV, respectively for the five characteristic peaks. In the high-resolution XPS spectra of P-CDs@BA (Fig. 3e–g), the N1s band can be decomposed into two peaks, CN at 396.6 eV and N–H at 401.5 eV, respectively [24,48]. The B1s band can be decomposed into three peaks, which are B–C at 192 eV, B2O3 at 193.4 eV and B–O at 194 eV [43,49] The P 2p band can be decomposed into two peaks, P–N at 134.8 eV and P–O/PO at 135.4 eV, respectively [47,50]. This is the same as the previous experimental results and further indicates the successful preparation of phosphorus-doped CDs and BA matrix composites.

From the bottom-up preparation, the formation of CDs presumably undergoes two processes: cross-linking and carbonization. The process of CDs formation has been discussed in previous work [30], and we deduce that the EDTA, PA and BA in this work undergo a process of cross-linking and polymerization at the beginning of the reaction, during which nanoparticles are formed. Afterward, during the process of increasing temperature, the nanoparticles partially carbonize at high temperature to form CDs, and the surface of the CDs will form interactions with the substrate so that the CDs will be embedded in the matrix.

To conclude, phosphorus-doped CDs and BA matrix composites were successfully prepared. The CDs are sphere-like nanoparticles centered on a carbon core with abundant groups on the surface and are uniformly dispersed in a borate matrix. It is hypothesized that the phosphorus doping makes the composite material with a unique time-dependent color-changing RTP emission, and the introduction of the BA matrix provides a rigidizing environment for the CDs and provides spatial confinement effects.

Further studies on the photophysical properties of P-CDs@BA composites were carried out. As shown in Fig. 4 , the prepared P-CDs@BA composite powder has no luminescent property in daylight, and when excited by a 365 nm UV lamp, the P-CDs@BA composite powder shows a 4 s afterglow visible to the naked eye, and the color gradually changes from yellow to green. Time-responsive color-changing RTP is extremely rare among the reported carbon dot-based room temperature phosphorescent materials. This unique property provides more possibilities for its application in various fields.

To investigate the luminescence behavior of P-CDs@BA composites, we verified our speculations step by step through a series of experiments. First, two materials, P-CD and CDs@BA, were obtained in the same way controlling the participation of PA and BA. Among them, P-CDs are black powder substances, that do not show any optical properties in solid state and show blue fluorescence (FL) when dissolved in water. However, CDs@BA showed green RTP properties. Therefore, we suggest that the luminescence properties of P-CDs@BA composites are closely related to the incorporation of BA and PA, which gives the composites RTP properties, and the phosphorus incorporation causes the time-responsive color-changing RTP emission properties of P-CDs@BA composites. This may be due to the introduction of the boric acid matrix which makes the carbon dots mosaic, on the one hand, the spatial restriction effect brought by the matrix makes the non-radiative decay caused by the collision of carbon dot particles and oxygen burst etc. suppressed. On the other hand, a certain number of hydrogen bonds will be formed between the matrix and the CDs surface groups and the matrix, forming a rigidized network environment which suppression of the vibrational rotation of molecular groups. The multi-limiting effect allows the non-radiative decay of the triplet excited state to be greatly limited, which in turn allows the P-CDs@BA composites to acquire RTP properties. The participation of phosphorus will lead to the creation of new luminescent centers in the CDs, and eventually the RTP properties of P-CDs@BA composites exhibit time-dependent color-change behavior due to the presence of multiple luminescent centers.

P-CDs@BA and CDs@BA composites were selected for further discussion. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the UV–vis absorption spectra of P-CDs@BA and CDs@BA show a more distinct absorption peak at around 350 nm for both, which is attributed to the π-π* and n-π* transitions of CO, CN and PO, etc [19,51]. The fluorescence spectra of P-CDs@BA and CDs@BA are shown in Fig. 5b and c. The emission wavelengths of CDs@BA were located around 400 nm at different excitation wavelengths, which did not change greatly and reflected the non-excitation dependence. However, the P-CDs@BA composites exhibit excitation dependence with a slight red-shift in emission wavelength with increasing excitation wavelength and strong fluorescence emission under 335 nm and 365 nm light excitation, which may be the result of multiple luminescence centers. As shown in Fig. 5d and e, with the change of excitation wavelength, there is no significant change in the emission wavelength in the phosphorescence spectrogram of CDs@BA composites, which is located around 500 nm. However, it is noteworthy that an obvious excitation dependence is reflected in the phosphorescence spectra of P-CDs@BA composites, and there is a significant red shift of the emitted light wavelength with increasing excitation wavelength, as well as a strong RTP emission at 525 nm and 573 nm under 335 nm and 365 nm excitation wavelengths, respectively. This suggests the existence of multiple species of luminescent centers in the P-CDs@BA composites, which leads to the nature of the P-CDs@BA composites to obtain temporal multicolor RTP properties. The International Commission on Illumination (CIE) chromaticity coordinates of P-CDs@BA composites are shown in Fig. 5f. The phosphorescence color of P-CDs@BA composites gradually shifts from yellow to green with time, which is consistent with the obtained experimental results of this color phenomenon.

To further investigate the time-dependent color-changing RTP properties of P-CDs@BA composites, RTP decay curves of P-CDs@BA and CDs@BA composites were obtained. As shown in Fig. 6 a, b and Table 2 , The average phosphorescence lifetime of P-CDs@BA composites was 114 ms at the emission wavelength of 525 nm under 335 nm optical excitation, and 57 ms at the emission wavelength of 573 nm under 365 nm optical excitation, respectively. It is worth noting that the intensity of yellow light is higher at 0.1 s, while the intensity of green light is higher at 0.2–1 s. The difference in phosphorescence lifetime and the difference in phosphorescence intensity further led to the P-CDs@BA composites possessing time-dependent properties, and eventually the P-CDs@BA composites acquired time-dependent color-changing RTP properties.

In summary, the P-CDs@BA composites acquire unique time-dependent color-changing RTP properties due to the participation of BA and PA. As shown in Fig. 7 , on the one hand, the introduction of the boric acid matrix makes the carbon dots embedded in it, and the spatial confinement effect brought by the matrix makes the non-radiative decay caused by the motion, collision and oxygen burst of the carbon dot particles be suppressed. At the same time, a certain number of hydrogen bonds are formed between the substrate and the CDs surface and the substrate, forming a rigidized network environment making the vibrational rotation of the molecular groups inhibited. This multi-limiting interaction synergistically allows the non-radiative decay of the triple excited states to be greatly limited, which in turn allows the P-CDs@BA composites to acquire RTP properties. On the other hand, the participation of phosphorus will lead to new luminescent centers in the CDs, and the multiple luminescent centers make the P-CDs@BA composites possess multi-color phosphorescent properties, while the difference in the luminescent lifetime and intensity of the luminescent centers makes them possess time-dependent properties. The combination of these two aspects results in a time-dependent color-changing RTP property of P-CDs@BA composites. As shown in Fig. 7, first, under the excitation of the UV light source, the material is excited to occur from the ground state (S0) to the unstable excited state (Sm) of the energy level transitions and then Sm transitions to lowest excited singlet state (S1) through internal conversion (IC), due to the existence of two different light-emitting centers, in the aftermath of two different paths of the ISC process occurs, the material from S1 to T1, and ultimately from T1 back to S0 and in the process emit light with different colors and lifetimes.

At present, fluorescent and phosphorescent anti-counterfeiting technologies have been used for anti-counterfeiting of various commodities such as tobacco and alcohol. In contrast, however, time-dependent color-changing RTP allows for multiple levels of anti-counterfeiting efforts. Based on the time-dependent color-changing room temperature phosphorescent properties of P-CDs@BA composites, its application in multi-level anti-counterfeiting was explored. As shown in Fig. 8 , using P-CDs@BA composite ink, we drew the butterfly and floral patterns by drawing them through missed cardboard prints. The anti-counterfeiting ink is evenly coated onto the paper surface, waiting for the solvent to evaporate to obtain the anti-counterfeiting patterns. After excitation by a 365 nm UV excitation light source, the butterfly and flower patterns show a change in afterglow from yellow to green with time. In this way, in addition to the pattern and light color, the color change of the afterglow after UV excitation brings a third anti-counterfeiting effect. The superposition of multiple effects makes the photoluminescence anti-counterfeiting technology achieve further improvement.

In summary, P-CDs@BA composites with unique time-dependent color-changing room-temperature phosphorescent properties were successfully synthesized by the one-pot method using EDTA, BA and PA as raw materials. The sphere-like CDs in the P-CDs@BA composites were uniformly dispersed in the BA matrix. The rigid environment provided by the hydrogen bonding interaction between the matrix and the CDs and the formation of the matrix inhibits the vibrational rotation of the surface groups, while the spatial confinement inhibits the motion, collision and oxygen burst of the CDs. The P-CDs@BA composites possess room temperature phosphorescent properties under the synergistic effect of multiple constraints. In addition, the phosphorus participation makes the P-CDs@BA composites possess multiple luminescent centers. The coexistence of multiple luminescent centers and the difference in the phosphor lifetime and luminescence intensity of the luminescent centers make the P-CDs@BA composites possess unique time-dependent color-changing properties. Finally, a specific example of the application of P-CDs@BA composites in the field of anti-counterfeiting is given based on their time-dependent color-changing RTP properties. The triple effect of graphics, color and time-dependent color change is superimposed to achieve multiple anti-counterfeiting effects. The present work provides ideas for the preparation of time-dependent color-changing carbon dot-based room temperature phosphorescent materials and their applications in the field of anti-counterfeiting.

Le Zhang: Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yang Wang: Investigation, Validation, Methodology. Zhentao Bian: Investigation, Conceptualization, Supervision. Sridhar Komarneni: Conceptualization, Supervision. Guangzhou Hu: Project administration, Resources, Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

J. Mater. Chem. B
